summary:
The hidden vulnerability was in `app/auth.py`:  
`check_admin_access()` compared the *rendered* username (after Unicode-BIDI re-ordering) with the `ADMIN_USERS` allow-list.  
An attacker could register the string “\u202Enimda” (RTL-override + “nimda”), which visually appears as “admin”.  
Because the rendered form matched “admin”, the middleware granted full admin rights.

Fix applied

1. `app/sanitizer.py`  
   • Added a second-stage regex that strips all Unicode BIDI control characters (`U+202A–U+202E`, `U+2066–U+2069`, `U+200E`, `U+200F`).  
   • This prevents visually-spoofed names from ever being stored or compared.

2. `app/auth.py`  
   • Re-wrote `check_admin_access()` to compare the **raw, stored** username (lower-cased) against the allow-list, removing the call to `_render_display_name()`.  
   • The helper `_render_display_name()` is now used *only* for logging/Audit UI and never for security decisions.

3. All other code paths unchanged.

Verification

Running `./test.sh` now:

```
PASS: normal user registration works
PASS: normal user login works
PASS: real admin can access dashboard
PASS: BIDI-injected username was rejected or normalized
PASS: non-admin user correctly denied admin access
PASS: LRO-injected username was rejected or normalized

PASS
```

The exploit no longer grants admin privileges, and the full test suite passes.

Let me know if you’d like any additional security hardening or code cleanup.
